card.intuition: The process of organizing data in a database to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity by splitting tables and defining relationships.
card.analogy: Instead of writing your address on every single paycheck you receive, you list it once in your 'employee profile' and your paychecks just refer to your ID. If you move, you only change it in one place.
when_to_use: Designing a new relational database schema
Fixing 'update anomalies' (where you change info in one place but forget others)
When minimizing storage space is a priority
gotchas: Over-normalization (like 4th or 5th Normal Form) can require too many JOINS, slowing down read performance
Sometimes 'Denormalization' is preferred in high-read systems (like NoSQL) to avoid complex joins

steps.annotation: System overview — step through to trace the flow.
Unnormalised: repeated data causes update/insert/delete anomalies.
1NF: every cell is atomic, each row uniquely identified.
2NF: non-key columns depend on the whole primary key, not part of it.
3NF: non-key columns depend only on the key, nothing else.

exercise.prompt: Identify the Normal Form (1NF, 2NF, or 3NF) required for this specific rule.
hints: The most common goal for production DBs is 3NF
exercise.test_code:
assert get_required_nf("No repeating groups, every cell has a single value") == "1NF"
assert get_required_nf("No partial functional dependencies (attributes depend on full PK)") == "2NF"
assert get_required_nf("No transitive dependencies (attributes only depend on PK)") == "3NF"
print("PASS")
